attributes.stem:
A 25-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a one-hour history of sudden clear fluid loss from her vagina while hanging washing. She is in her second pregnancy at 35 weeks' gestation, with one previous birth. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. She reports continued fetal movements and denies pain or contractions. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy vitamins.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 80/min, blood pressure 110/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. The fetus is in a longitudinal lie with cephalic presentation, and the head is not engaged. There is no uterine tenderness.

Speculum examination reveals no cord prolapse with clear fluid pooling in the posterior fornix with a closed cervix. Amnicator test is positive and microscopy shows ferning pattern. Group B Streptococcus swab has been collected. Cardiotocograph shows fetal heart rate 150/min with no decelerations or uterine contractions.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Preterm labour and associated placental abruption
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of preterm prelabour rupture of membranes (PPROM). Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes is diagnosed when membrane rupture occurs before 37 weeks gestation in the absence of labour. The diagnosis is confirmed by the triad of pooling of amniotic fluid on speculum examination, positive Amnicator test, and ferning pattern on microscopy.

First stage of labour is excluded by the absence of regular uterine contractions and a closed cervix on examination.

Prelabour rupture of membranes refers to membrane rupture at term (≥37 weeks), whereas this patient is preterm at 35 weeks gestation.

Chorioamnionitis is excluded by the absence of fever, uterine tenderness, fetal tachycardia, or offensive vaginal discharge. However, it is an important complication to monitor for in the setting of PPROM.

Preterm labour and associated placental abruption would typically present with painful contractions and vaginal bleeding, neither of which are present in this case.

The management of PPROM involves careful assessment of gestational age, monitoring for signs of infection or labour, administration of antibiotics and corticosteroids, and consideration of transfer to a tertiary centre if delivery is likely to be required.
learningPoints: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes (PPROM) is diagnosed when membrane rupture occurs before 37 weeks gestation in the absence of labour.
Key diagnostic features of PPROM include pooling of amniotic fluid on speculum examination, positive Amnicator test, and ferning pattern on microscopy.
The absence of fever, uterine tenderness, and fetal tachycardia helps exclude chorioamnionitis.
references: Prelabor Rupture of Membranes: ACOG Practice Bulletin, Number 217. Obstet Gynecol. 2020 Mar;135(3):e80-e97. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32080050/
